replacement_html:
5.0 April 17 2026 Update — Author-Initiated Kill-Shot Demotions

On 2026-04-17 the dome author edited docs/killshot.html (commit 5021eec) to demote two of six kill-shot rows — Sydney-Perth (CONFIRMED (globe falsified) → STRUCTURAL SUPPORT) and Polaris (CONFIRMED (globe falsified) → SUPPORTIVE ONLY) — and replaced the binary-knockout falsification rule with a softer pre-registration-required rule. This is good-faith self-correction in the direction of accuracy, and we credit it; but the demoted rows are precisely the two post-window rows on a six-row page, and the CSS styling still renders both as green-check "confirmed" badges to a reader scanning by color.

Credit where due. The dome author publicly, with a dated GitHub commit, walked back two of the stronger rhetorical claims on his own Kill-Shot page. "✅ CONFIRMED (globe falsified)" is a very strong phrase; downgrading it to "✅ STRUCTURAL SUPPORT" and "✅ SUPPORTIVE ONLY" is closer to what those two rows actually establish on their own. The commit (5021eec, 2026-04-17 07:39 EDT on John09289/predictions) is public and auditable. This is the kind of iterative tightening we want to see.

What changed (verbatim, verified against the live repository on 2026-04-18). The dome's Kill-Shot table rows now read:

Row Before 2026-04-17 After 2026-04-17
Sydney-Perth rail distance ✅ CONFIRMED (globe falsified) ✅ STRUCTURAL SUPPORT
Polaris elevation at 35.9°N ✅ CONFIRMED (globe falsified) ✅ SUPPORTIVE ONLY

The other four kill-shot rows (Aug 12 2026 eclipse EBR; NMP drift rate by 2027; SAA African cell field strength; JFK–LHR aetheric slipstream) all remained "⏳ PENDING" and were not materially changed in this edit.

Why the pattern matters. The two demoted rows are precisely the two rows whose test windows had already closed (the Indian Pacific railway distance, surveyed 1912–1917; the Polaris sextant measurement attributed to 2025). The four unchanged rows are those whose test windows have not yet closed. Demoting post-window claims while holding pre-window claims unchanged is a rational author move — post-window is where counter-evidence is already available — but it is also exactly the pattern we would predict if the original "CONFIRMED" label had been oversold on the retrospective rows. The sequencing is itself evidence that the original binary-knockout framing was not a careful statement of what those two pieces of evidence could bear.

The falsification-rule rewrite (same commit). The page's falsification rule section was both renamed and rewritten:

  • Heading, before: "The Falsification Rule"
  • Heading, after: "How To Read This Page Correctly"
  • Body, before: "If any single test confirms, the globe model is falsified. If any single test fails, the dome model is falsified. There is no aggregation and no correlation required. Each domain stands entirely on its own as a definitive physical contradiction."
  • Body, after: "A clean result only counts as a major discriminator if the test is genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation. Supportive, calibration-heavy, or non-discriminating rows should not be turned into global cosmology knockouts by slogan alone. Use audit.html for claim class and review-response.html for current anti-strawman scope."

The page also picked up a new tagline: "Some rows are stronger than others; this page is not a license to flatten those differences."

The original rule was one of the strongest rhetorical commitments on the dome site — a binary-knockout rule against which our Part 5 was directly pointed. Its replacement is a considerably weaker rule. "Pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, not already covered by a competing explanation" is not a kill-shot rule; it is a methodology-of-good-evidence rule. Under the old rule, the author committed to let any single confirmed row falsify the globe. Under the new rule, he reserves the right to discount any individual row that does not meet the tighter criteria — and the only rows that stand under that rule are roughly the ones we have already assessed as "Std Model Explains" or "Refuted by Data." The rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test — which is the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do.

A natural dome-defender objection. A defender might argue that the labels were always "structural support" in substance, and the 2026-04-17 edit merely brings the wording into alignment. If that were so, the falsification rule — which was framed in explicit binary-knockout terms — would not have needed to be rewritten at the same time; the rule and the labels moved together. The substance changed along with the label.

The CSS-class presentation mismatch. Both demoted rows retain the CSS class status-confirmed, which styles the badge green and applies the ✅ check glyph. On the live page at the time of writing (2026-04-18), "✅ STRUCTURAL SUPPORT" and "✅ SUPPORTIVE ONLY" render with the same green-check styling used elsewhere on the dome site for unambiguous confirmations. A reader who scans by badge colour alone still sees two green-checked rows alongside the four pending rows. The wording concedes the demotion; the presentation still registers them as confirmations. This is almost certainly unintentional, but it is worth flagging because the visual scan is how most readers actually process a status table of this kind.

What this means for Part 5's tests. Our Test 1 (Sydney–Perth) and Test 2 (Polaris altitude) already argued that these rows do not falsify the globe — our verdicts were "Misleading" and "Not Demonstrated" respectively. The April 17 demotion moves the author's own labels closer to our verdict, which we acknowledge. The demotion does not, however, vacate the underlying critiques: calibration to a known railway distance (Test 1) remains calibration whether the badge reads "CONFIRMED" or "STRUCTURAL SUPPORT," and a 0.27° Polaris excess within measurement noise (Test 2) remains unresolved whether the badge reads "CONFIRMED" or "SUPPORTIVE ONLY." The tests below are unchanged in substance; what has changed is that the dome author and this review now agree those two rows are not kill-shots.

What was not demoted. The four pending rows retain the original claim structure in substance — specific numerical predictions against specific mainstream alternatives, with commit-dated expectation windows. Our analyses of those rows (Tests 3–6 below) stand unchanged. Of particular note: the NMP drift-rate row (Test 6) — the one row whose own monitoring currently reports 39.9% error — was not demoted in this edit. The demotion targeted the rhetorically overstated but not-yet-falsified retrospective rows, not the row that is actually under-performing on its self-set metric.

Companion concession on the OTS canary. A related, roughly 20-minute-earlier commit on the same page (6e06efb, same day) replaced the AI-Auditor block's assertion "All predictions are Bitcoin-timestamped via OpenTimestamps" with "OpenTimestamps proves snapshot existence by time; claim-level prospectivity still needs item-by-item audit." This is partial acknowledgement of our critique but not the structural fix — the prediction documents still have not been separated from the observation file and independently timestamped. Full analysis in the Timestamp Error tab.

intro_patch.note: Part 5 intro currently quotes the OLD falsification rule verbatim. After this expansion lands, the quote is stale and should be updated. Two specific patches:

patch_1.location: Part 5 first paragraph (currently: 'This part uses the dome's own: binary tests with the dome's stated rule that failure of any single test falsifies the model.')
patch_1.proposed_replacement: This part uses the dome's own framework: six binary tests drawn from the dome's Kill-Shot page. The page's stated rule was rewritten on 2026-04-17 (see Section 5.0); both the pre- and post-rewrite versions of the rule are evaluated below.

patch_2.location: Part 5 second paragraph, the bold-rule quote (currently: "If any single test confirms, globe is falsified. If any single test fails, dome is falsified.")
patch_2.proposed_replacement: The dome's kill-shot page presents six binary tests. Through 2026-04-17 the page framed them under a bold rule — "If any single test confirms, globe is falsified. If any single test fails, dome is falsified." — which was replaced on that date with a softer pre-registration-required rule (see Section 5.0 for the dated before/after). As of the updated page, two rows are labelled STRUCTURAL SUPPORT / SUPPORTIVE ONLY; three are PENDING; one (NMP drift rate) self-reports as passing at 39.9% error — though only by widening the tolerance to 50% after predictions missed; and the sixth (JFK-LHR) is PENDING (insufficient data). We also add a seventh test — Stellar Parallax (Gaia DR3) — which the dome framework implies via WIN-017 but has never explicitly confronted against Gaia's 1.8-billion-star catalog.

patch_3.location: Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table (ks-verdict-summary) — update the 'Dome Says' column for rows 1 and 2
patch_3.proposed_replacement: Row 1 'Dome Says' cell: change Confirmed to Structural Support (demoted 2026-04-17). Row 2 'Dome Says' cell: change Confirmed to Supportive Only (demoted 2026-04-17). This keeps the table a faithful snapshot of the author's current labels while preserving the dated record via the 'demoted 2026-04-17' tag.

commit_5021eec.net_effect: Two badge demotions (Sydney-Perth, Polaris) + falsification-rule rewrite + new tagline + CSS classes unchanged on demoted rows.

commit_6e06efb.net_effect: AI-Auditor block OTS line changed from 'All predictions are Bitcoin-timestamped via OpenTimestamps' to 'OpenTimestamps proves snapshot existence by time; claim-level prospectivity still needs item-by-item audit.' Partial OTS concession — NOT the canary fix (still timestamps the wrong side of the ledger). Covered fully under EXP-189 on the Timestamp Error tab; cross-referenced here.

commit_a57e731.net_effect: Cosmetic — 'steelman' language replaced with 'strongest-evidence' throughout. No semantic change. Not separately documented in replacement_html.

holes_addressed: No existing Part 5 reference to the 2026-04-17 demotions — as of the pre-expansion state, our Part 5 still quotes the OLD falsification rule verbatim as the dome's stated rule, and the Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table's 'Dome Says' column still reads 'Confirmed' for rows 1 and 2. This is now factually stale; a hostile reader could use the stale quote to dismiss our review as out of date.

No preservation of the dated record — without this block, the pre-2026-04-17 'CONFIRMED (globe falsified)' wording is preserved only in GitHub commit history on the dome author's repo. Our review needs its own dated record so that future readers encounter the transition in our narrative rather than having to reconstruct it from another repository.

No flag on the CSS-class mismatch — visually, the demoted rows still render with the green-check 'status-confirmed' styling. A reader scanning by badge colour sees two confirmations where the wording now says 'structural support' / 'supportive only'.
No commentary on the post-window / pre-window sequencing pattern — the fact that the two demoted rows are exactly the two rows whose test windows have closed.
new_evidence_added: Verbatim before/after table of the two badge demotions, with commit SHA 5021eec and 2026-04-17 07:39 EDT timestamp
Verbatim before/after block for the falsification-rule rewrite (heading + body + new tagline)
Observation: the two demoted rows = the two post-window rows; the four pending rows are all pre-window
Observation: 'status-confirmed' CSS class retained on both demoted rows → green-check badge color unchanged
Cross-reference to EXP-189 / Timestamp Error tab for the companion OTS concession in commit 6e06efb

anticipated_objections.objection: You are punishing good-faith clarification — the author updated his own status labels publicly with a dated commit, and your response is to turn that into more material against him.
anticipated_objections.response: We lead with the charitable framing and explicitly credit the self-correction as 'the kind of iterative tightening we want to see.' The purpose of the dated block is not to score points but to preserve the dated record so that a future reader encountering our Part 5 understands what the rows used to say, what they now say, and when the change happened. If the author later argues our Part 5 was attacking straw-men, the dated block gives him and us a common reference for what the strong-knockout claim actually was and when it was walked back.

anticipated_objections.objection: The labels were always 'structural support' in substance and the 2026-04-17 edit just makes the wording match the substance.
anticipated_objections.response: If that were the author's position, the falsification rule — framed in explicit binary-knockout terms ('If any single test confirms, globe is falsified') — would not have needed to be rewritten in the same commit; the rule and the labels moved together. The substance changed along with the label. Either the earlier binary rule was wrong (in which case the demotion is real), or the earlier rule reflected the real substance (in which case the demotion is real). There is no reading on which both the old labels and the old rule were always-already consistent with a 'structural support' substance.

anticipated_objections.objection: Four of six kill-shots remain PENDING — you should not suggest the pending rows were demoted; they were not.
anticipated_objections.response: We do not. The replacement_html explicitly records that the other four rows were unchanged and draws attention to the fact that the unchanged rows are exactly the rows whose test windows have not yet closed. This distinction is load-bearing for the section's argument.

anticipated_objections.objection: Our Timestamp Error tab critiques the OTS wrong-side error. The author has now partially conceded it. You should close the tab, not keep it open.
anticipated_objections.response: The partial concession in commit 6e06efb — 'OpenTimestamps proves snapshot existence by time; claim-level prospectivity still needs item-by-item audit' — is acknowledgement at the prose level, not the operational fix. The structural fix (a predictions-only document, independently timestamped before data arrives) remains unimplemented. EXP-189 updates the Timestamp Error tab to record the partial concession while keeping the underlying critique live.

anticipated_objections.objection: A CSS class name is a nitpick; the wording is what matters.
anticipated_objections.response: Agreed that wording is primary — which is why the dated before/after block is the centrepiece of this subsection. The CSS note is secondary and framed that way ('almost certainly unintentional'). The reason it is worth mentioning at all is that a status-table element's visual encoding is part of how most readers parse it; wording-demotion-without-styling-demotion is a known pattern in dashboard design where the presentation layer hasn't caught up with the content layer.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author noticed, on his own initiative, that calling Sydney-Perth and Polaris 'CONFIRMED (globe falsified)' was stronger than those two pieces of evidence could bear, and he walked both claims back on 2026-04-17 with a dated public commit. He also replaced the binary-knockout rule with a more defensible 'pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, not already covered by a competing explanation' standard. Both of these are genuine improvements on the version of the page our Part 5 was written against. Our failure mode is to treat this as unambiguous win material rather than as accurate rhetorical tightening; we should not, and our dated block explicitly credits it.

Breaking News — existing entry dated 2026-04-17 covers the Timestamp Error side; a parallel Kill-Shot demotion entry may be warranted, human decision
newsworthy: Dome Author Demotes Two of Six Kill-Shots, Rewrites the Falsification Rule. On 2026-04-17 the dome author moved Sydney-Perth and Polaris from 'CONFIRMED (globe falsified)' to 'STRUCTURAL SUPPORT' / 'SUPPORTIVE ONLY' and replaced the binary-knockout rule with a softer pre-registration-required rule. The two demoted rows are exactly the two rows whose test windows had closed; the four not-yet-tested rows are unchanged. We credit the self-correction and preserve the dated record.
tldr_update_note: This expansion adds a new Section 5.0 whose summary carries its own ps-tldr (punchline: two of six kill-shots demoted on 2026-04-17, but only the post-window rows, and the CSS styling still says 'confirmed'). It does not replace the TLDRs of any existing Part 5 section. However, Part 5's top-level intro is affected — the intro_patch field documents the specific text changes needed.
notes:
Primary output is replacement_html (the new Section 5.0 dated-event block) for insertion between the Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table and Test 1. Secondary output is intro_patch, describing specific text substitutions needed in the Part 5 intro paragraphs and Verdict Summary table rows so that the pre-expansion quote of the OLD falsification rule stops being stale. The decider should apply the intro_patch AT THE SAME TIME as integrating the new 5.0 block so that the dome's current-and-previous wording is consistent across the section. Charitable-framing discipline: credit the self-correction, preserve the dated record, do not claim the unchanged rows were demoted, and let the author's own pattern (post-window rows demoted, pre-window rows unchanged) speak for itself. Analyst chose a dedicated subsection over weaving into existing tests because (a) this is a single dated event with several components that read better together, (b) the body and the rule rewrite affect every test below and the reader needs the context before encountering the per-test analyses, and (c) it allows a single cross-reference target for Part 1 / Breaking News / EXP-189. ) per BUILD-AND-CHANGE.md spec.
